It is an example of God’s grace that, after performing many miracles to punish sin, he would work one more to prevent it. Twelve rods or staves were to be brought in. It is likely that they were the staves which the princes used as symbols of their authority; old, dry staves that had no sap in them. They were to expect that the rod of the tribe, or prince, whom God chose for the priesthood, should bud and blossom. Moses did not object that the matter was already settled; he did not try to decide it but left the case before the Lord.

While all the other rods remained as they were, Aaron's rod became a living branch. In some places there were buds, in others blossoms, in others fruit, all at the same time; this was miraculous. Thus Aaron was shown to be under Heaven’s special blessing. Fruitfulness is the best evidence of a divine call; and the plants that God has set, and the branches cut from them, will flourish. This rod was preserved to take away the people's murmurings so they might not die. God’s design, in all his providences and memorials of them, is to take away sin. Christ was manifested to take away sin. Christ is expressly called a rod out of the stem of Jesse; there was little prospect, according to human views, that he should ever flourish. But the dry rod revived and blossomed to the confusion of his adversaries. The people cry, ‘Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish!’ This was the language of a complaining people, arguing with God’s judgments, which they brought upon themselves through their own pride and stubbornness. It is very wicked to be upset against God when we are in affliction, and in our distress to sin even more. If we die, if we perish, it is because of ourselves, and the blame will be on our own heads. When God judges, he will overcome and will oblige the most stubborn opponents to confess their folly. And how great are our mercies that we have a clearer and better system established upon better promises!
